 ince   the   release   of the   second   edition   of he   nterlinear   ebrew-
 reek-nglish   ible,   ible   students   around   the world   have   found   this
 work   to be a valuable   resource   for gaining   insight into the original lan-
guages   of cripture.   ecause   an   interlinear   ible   is a helpful   aid   for
those who   may   not have had a formal   course   of study in the ebrew   and
 reek   languages,   demand   for such   an   accessible   tool as this has grown
steadily   despite its sometimes   limited   availability.
he   addition   of trong’s   numbers   to the second   edition   has   enabled
those   with   little   or   no   prior knowledge   of reek   or   ebrew   to access
 a   wealth   of language   reference   works   keyed   to   trong’s   haustive
 oncordance—reek/ebrew   dictionaries,   analytical   leicons,   concor-
dances,   word   studies,   and   others.   oreover,   the inclusion   of the iteral
 ranslation   of the ible   has offered   readers   at-a-glance   insight into the
 roots   of many   biblical   words.   or   eample   biblical   names   reveal   rich
meanings   (e.g. the ethinim   are   temple-slaves).   he   names   of biblical
places   convey   more   (e.g.   ibroth-attaavah   means   “he   raves   of
 ust’). fficial positions   are eplained   (captain of the guard is more   lit-
 erally rendered   “chief of the eecutioners”).
 he   volumes   that make   up this set are   an   eact   reprint of the second
 edition   and   are   being   produced   under   license   from   the original   editor
 and   copyright   holder.   ecause   it   is   an   eact   reprint,   no   editorial
changes—additions   or   corrections—have   been   made.   s   such,   it will

 this affordable   reprint is offered   in the hope that the reissue   of this clas-
sic reference   tool will meet   an important   need   for all who   seek a better
understanding   of cripture.

fter nearly forty centuries   of desperate   failure, has atan,   aided   and
cheerfully abetted   by deceitful   and desperately   wicked   men   (er. ),
finally succeeded   in destroying   the written   word   of od, the ible, as a
single-voiced witness   to his great arch-enemy,   our od   and   avior esus
hrist   as the old evil, like a sleight-of-hand   shell-game   artist, finally
brought   us to the point where   we   are searching desperately   for the true
word   of od   re we   to believe   that it cannot   now   be intact after all this
 time, having been run through   the shredder of unholy hands   and heads
 et it not be said! or not only has od warned   us that, “an   shall not live
 by bread alone, but by every word   that proceeds out of the mouth   of od,”
 (eut.  att. ), but od has provided us with the very words that we
are   to live by, and   e   has most   certainly also preserved   for us the very
words   by which   we   are   to live. lse, how   could   e   plainly say, “nd   if
 anyone hears y words and does not believe,   do not judge him . .. the word
which  spoke is that which will judge him in the last day,” (ohn ,)
 od   has given   us   is   life-giving   words   in words   which   all men   may
receive   and believe.   rom   the least intelligent to the most   brilliant, men
from all backgrounds   and in all lands and languages must be able to hear,
understand, believe and obey else the ivine purpose   cannot be fulfilled.
n   order   to   leave   every   person   without   ecuse,   od   the   oly   pirit
breathed   out through the divinely chosen   penmen   words   in common   use
 among   the   ebrew   people,   and,   later,   words   commonly   in   use   by the
 reek-speaking   world.   owever,   since   few   today   read   ebrew   and
reek,   and   since   the   criptdures   are   intended   for every   nation   and
 tongue, a translation into the common   language of each person   is needed.
 his yndale wrote in his preface, “which thing moved   me   to translate the
 ew   estament.   ecause    had   perceived   by eperience   that   it was
 impossible   to establish   the lay people   in any truth   ecept   the   cripture
were   plainly laid before their eyes in their mother tongue, that they might
see   the   process,   order   and   meaning   of the tet.”   yndale   labored   to
translate   so   that   “every   plowboy   might   understand,”   and   such   a great
spiritual revolution   followed   that . . reen, the noted nglish historian,
felt compelled   to write,   “o   greater   moral   change   ever   passed   over   a
 nation   than passed over ngland   in the latter part of the reign of ueen
 lizabeth.   ngland became   the people of a ook, and that ook   was   the
ible. twas read by every class of people. nd the effect was amazing. he
whole   moral tone   of the nation   was   changed.”

ith these considerations in mind, and also with much fear and
 trembling before our majestic and awesome od, we have sought to
 provide in he nterlinear ebrew-reek-nglish ible both the original
 od-breathed   ebrew, ramaic and reek words (as incorporated in the
 tets received by the vast majority of  ible-believing peoples), and two
literal nglish translations of these  words. ur  purpose in doing so is that
the reader and student  of this work might  be led to “receive in meekness the
 implanted word, which is able to save your souls” (ames ). t now is
 submitted to you in the hope that the lmighty od   who enabled us to
 conceive   and to eecute this monumental task will also enable you to grow
in grace and in the knowledge of our ord esus  hrist by the use of it.
       
his   work,   we   believe,   contains   all the   ebrew   and   ramaic   words
 which have been preserved for us by the asoretes,   and which   in total has
 come   to be known   as the   asoretic   et.   his   work   also   contains   the
 reek   words   as   printed   in the   tephens   dition   of ,   which   has
 become   knownas   the etus   eceptus,   or, eceived et, for the past four
centuries.   e   have not followed   the order of the ebrew   ible (he aw,
 he   rophets,   he   ritings),   but the order of the nglish   ible.
 hy did we use these particular tets t is simply because these are the
 only tets which can justly be designated as ‘received’ tets. n worldwide
 acceptance   they tower   so far above   any other original ebrew   or reek
tets that there is no doubt but what they must be used in a work   such as
 this   is, a work   that   is intended   to become a standard   work   for all the
nglish-speaking   peoples   in   the   world.   hey   are   the   “eceived   ets’
because   no other tet has been   able to win   the adherence   of any group
powerful   enough   to displace   either the asoretic   or the eceived   et
from   their place as the standard   by which   all others   are   measured.
ince there will be separate prefaces supplied with each volume   of this
 work, we will confine our remarks here to matters concerning the ebrew
and nglish printed in this volume. he ebrew tet in the ld estament
volumes   of this work was   first typeset in  by the ritish and oreign
 ible   ociety,   and   it is from   the   asoretic   tet.   t was   chosen   over   other
 beautiful   ebrew   tets   simply   because   it provided,   for the   most   part,
 adequate   room   for the interlineary   addition   of the   nglish   equivalents
under   the original words.
 he   nglish,   both   that   under   the   ebrew   words   and   that   in   the
 translation   on   the side,   constitutes   the original translation   work   of ay .
 reen,   r.,   improved   by scholars   on   a   oard   of eview,   and   from   a
 volunteer   ditorial   oard.   or   purposes   of fiing   responsibility,   the
nglish   translations   are both those of ay . reen,   r., since   he was   the
 sole judge of what changes would, or would not, be allowed   in either the
nglish equivalents placed under the ebrew words, or the translation in
the column   at the side of the page.

.
 his being the only complete interlineary  ible in eistence, we give our
concept of what this work is, and   what it is not
. his nterlinear ible is a ible in every sense of  the word,  having both
 the original words, and two   nglish translations for nglish-speaking
 people. ome   may use this ible in order to read the  ible in the original
 languages others may read only the nglish translation, referring to the
 original words only in order to further investigate  the meaning in leicons
 or other reference works. t is our   firm   belief   that all the benefits
promised within  the ible itself may fairly be epected from the reading
 studying of this ible.
 . t is the most literal nglish translation every produced, certainly the
only one   with literal meanings   placed under the original words.   till, it is
 not in a true sense   an absolutely   literal representation   of the   ebrew   or
reek   words.   o   begin   with,   no   foreign   language   could   adequately
capture   all the   fullness   of epression   of either   the   ebrew   or   reek
 languages. n the ebrew particularly, it being a pictorially-based language,
 it is impossible for the nglish translator to bring out in nglish   the many
 shades   of thought   which   are   found   in the ebrew   ible   (¢.g. when   the
 translation   reads   “meek”,   this will hardly convey   to you   that   the word   is
 derived from a root signifying to “‘afflict” and when you read “delight”, you
 will not realize that the original word pictures out a bending down   toward
 the object being delighted   in).
 , tnot only gives the nglish reader insight into the literal meanings   of
 the   original languages,   but opens   to the understanding   many   idiomatic
 epressions.   owever,   at this   point,   it must   be   admitted   that   the   final
 product   is somewhat   of a hybrid   production.   n   the one   hand,   it is not
 possible to epress all the ebrew   idiom in nglish. n the other hand, if
 all idioms   were   attempted   in nglish,   many   nglish   readers   would   not
 always   receive   the sense,   being   unfamiliar   with   the background   of the
 idiomatic   epression.   t is for this reason   that two nglish translations   are
sometimes   provided,   one   under   each word   of the original, it being more
 literal and more   idiomatic   at times and the other on the side, giving   amore
 standard   treatment   of translation   so that the reader   might   more   easily
 collect the sense of the entire contet. here is another reason   for the lack
 of epression of some   of the idiom of the ebrew   language, and that is the
limitation in space with which   the translator was faced in this format. he
 cost of resetting   the ebrew   to fit a fully literal translation   into   nglish
 would   have been   so great that   the average   serious   ible   student   would
have found the final cost to be prohibitive. lso this work being intended
 for general use among   all nglish-understanding peoples, it was   necessary
 to avoid   epressions   which   could   only be comprehended   by the more
 advanced   ebrew scholars. t is hoped that this work as you now   see it will
 be useful to all classes of ible readers, whatever may be their educational

ievel, and that it will be the most useful ible  ever produced in the matter
 of conveying spiritual truths and insights to the  reader.
 . his ible should   prove to be a word book of considerable value for
the  study of iblical vocabulary, both ebrew and nglish.
 . t is intended   to be   a reader,   both   for those   who   are   reading   the
 nglish only, and for those who   desire to read the original and at the same
 time   to have   help in understanding   the meaning   of each   word.   t will
provide a much more   rapid-reading ebrew   ible than any that has been

 serve   as   a leicon   (dictionary).

 ebrew   or reek   grammar.
 . t is not intended   to be a substitute for the person,   in-depth study of
the   original.   tudents   of the   word   should   continue   to make   their own
studies, using every   reference   work   available   to them.   ach   one   should

. his work   should be of considerable   use to pastors and teachers.   o
 those who   have been   trained   in the original iblical languages   this work
 will be a refresher. t should help the trained student to remain   sharp and
fresh in his recognition of the meanings   of all the words in any contet. o
 the   untrained   pastor   or   teacher,   it should   be realized   that without   some
 knowledge   of the literal meanings   of the ebrew,   ramaic   and   reek

words.
. ecause   all the nearly nineteen   hundred   ebrew   root words   and
the seven   hundred   and fifty or so reek   root words   are here,   along with
 nglish   equivalents,   this work   is ideal   for the   use   of those   who   must

 . he   nterlinear   ible   is the fulfillment   of a long-recognized   need.
 artin   uther   is reported   as   saying   that   he   would   not   part   with   his
 knowledge   of ebrew   for untold gold. et the vast majority   of nglish-
speaking   people   have   been   shut   out   of this precious   knowledge,   not

sands   of interlineary ew estaments have been distributed in the past
century, is the  desire for a complete interlineary ible. nd this obviously

ebrew and reek languages in depth. ntil now, however, the
formidable task of producing such an immeasureably useful ible had

 here has been   some progress for several centuries. irst, for the
purpose of creating study tools of the magnitude that the ible necessi-
 tates, there must   have been   provided a standard translation in nglish.

. he nterlinear ible is up-to-date,   having used the latest leicons

 t should be kept in mind   in the use of this word   that there   is a wide
difference   in time and culture   and language,   not only between   ebrew
and   reek,   but also between   those   languages,   times   and   cultures,   and
 those   of our   day. n rendering   the iblical   languages   into   nglish   we
particularly found   it difficult   to deal with   the following

‘word’ or ‘command of  ehovah ‘lifting the  heads’   rather  than  counting,

 speech in literal form, and at the same time convey the meaning to the
nglish reader.
 . nterpretation. here is always interpretation in rendering one
 language into another, and it is necessary to consider the entire contet
 before making a translation. here   may be differences   of opinion as to
what the  contet teaches, but the purpose of this work has been to give
 e orgs of the original language as we interpreted it after lengthy
 study.
 . ntranslated   ords. here   are some   ebrew   words which   are not
 translated   in this work.   or instance,   “i an untranslated sign defining
 the direct object of a verb, and   at places,   ךשא   , the relative   particle,

 . mplied   ords.   here   are   many   instances   in which   the verb, or
 other   words,   are   implied   within   the   ebrew   word,   either   by   the
 sentence   structure,   the   synta,   or   the   contet.   n   such   cases   the
translator   has supplied   the word,   even   though   it is not represented   by
ebrew   characters. n these instances the word supplied has been put in
parentheses   so that the reader   will know   that they were   supplied by the
translator.   n the marginal   translation   these   supplied   words   may   be in
italic type.
 . unctuation   and   apitalization.   t should   be realized   that in the
 original   iblical   languages,   both   ebrew   and   reek,   all letters   were
 capital letters, as we   think of them.   herefore   in the use of capitals and
small   letters, as we   do in modern-day   nglish,   any capital letter can   be
 counted   as supplied. apital letters have been used in this work for the
following   for the beginning   of all sentences   for the beginning   word in
 all speeches   for specific   events   or places,   such   as, he   assover,   he
 east of abernacles,   he egeb,   he iver (either ile, or uphrates),
 etc. and we have also capitalized all pronouns which we deem   to refer to
the eity, whether od the ather, od the on, or od the oly pirit.
t should   also be noted   that there was   no punctuation   in the original
 manuscripts,   either in the ebrew   or reek.   n the ebrew   tet which
 we   are   using for this volume,   the asoretes   (c.    ad.) have   placed
 certain   punctuation   marks   for the benefit of the ebrew   reader. n this
work we   have generally followed the asoretic   punctuation,   keeping in
mind   that   punctuation   is an   aid   to   the   reader.   or   this   reason   the
 punctuation may seen   strange to the nglish reader at first, but since the
 reader is reading the nglish in ebrew   order (from right to left), and is
 oftentimes   reading   very   literally,   the   reader   will   be   aided   by the
 punctuation   as   it appears   under   the   ebrew   words.   n   the   nglish
 translation in the margin we   have followed modern   nglish punctuation
 rules, our   authority being   he   hicago   anual   of tyle.

n regard to the use of question marks and the indication of interrogation,
 it should be noted that a question is often indicated in the ebrew   at the
 beginning   of a sentence,   sometimes   by interrogative   words,   or at times
